target.title: fix(auto-reply): scope foreground reply fence per inbound message
target.body:
## Summary

When two users concurrently @mention the agent in a group chat with `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "automatic"`, both agent runs complete successfully — but only the reply to the latest message is delivered. The reply to the first user is silently cancelled by the foreground reply fence because the fence key was scoped per session target rather than per inbound message.

## Fix

In `src/auto-reply/dispatch.ts`, `resolveForegroundReplyFenceKey` now includes the inbound `MessageSid` in the fence composite key. When available, different inbound messages get independent fences and cannot cancel each other. Falls back to per-target scoping when no inbound message id is present.

**Before:**
```ts
return JSON.stringify([
  "foreground", channel, accountId, sessionKey, chatType, target
]);
```

**After:**
```ts
const inboundMsgId = finalized.MessageSid ?? finalized.MessageSidFirst;
return JSON.stringify([
  "foreground", channel, accountId, sessionKey, chatType, target,
  ...(inboundMsgId ? [inboundMsgId] : [])
]);
```

This means User A's reply (fence key includes MessageSid-A) and User B's reply (fence key includes MessageSid-B) are in separate fences — neither can suppress the other.

## Files changed

- `src/auto-reply/dispatch.ts`: +7 lines

## Related

Fixes #92186

### Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Concurrent group @mentions in `automatic` reply mode silently drop all replies except the newest one, because the foreground reply fence scopes per-session-target instead of per-inbound-message
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  cd /path/to/openclaw
  node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```console
  $ node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts
  ✓ dispatch > ... (existing dispatch tests pass)
  ```
  The fence key now includes the inbound MessageSid. Two concurrent dispatches from different inbound messages produce different fence keys, so neither delivery is suppressed by the other. When MessageSid is unavailable (e.g., cron/system events), the fence falls back to per-target scoping.
- **Observed result after fix**: Replies to distinct inbound messages in the same group chat are no longer suppressed by each other. Each inbound message gets its own independent fence.
- **What was not tested**: Real multi-user WhatsApp group with concurrent @mentions (requires live WhatsApp deployment); automated regression for group-chat fence behavior

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Fix heartbeat reasoning payload selection
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #92260.

This fixes heartbeat main-reply selection so reasoning payloads are not delivered as the visible heartbeat reply when `includeReasoning` is unset. The selector now skips flagged reasoning payloads (`isReasoning: true`) and the same common reasoning text forms already recognized by the shared reply-payload reasoning detector, including legacy `Reasoning:` / `Thinking...` forms and blockquoted reasoning.

The runner still preserves the explicit opt-in path: when `agents.defaults.heartbeat.includeReasoning: true`, reasoning-only responses continue to deliver through the separate reasoning delivery path instead of being treated as empty replies.

## Patch-level comparison

I checked for competing open PRs before opening this: none were open for #92260 when this PR was prepared. The closest competing patch was closed PR #92262.

Compared with #92262, this branch is intentionally different in a few ways:

- It avoids adding a new public `ResolveHeartbeatReplyPayloadOptions` export through `src/plugin-sdk/reply-runtime.ts`.
- It handles flagged reasoning and the existing prefixed/blockquote reasoning forms recognized by `isReasoningReplyPayload`.
- It adds runner-level regression coverage proving default `includeReasoning` suppression on the actual heartbeat send path, not only selector unit tests.
- It preserves and tests the opt-in `includeReasoning: true` behavior for reasoning-only responses, including the regression caught during autoreview.

Maintainers should feel free to prefer another branch if it fits the intended API direction better. I kept the comparison explicit so the delta is reviewable side-by-side with #92262.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Heartbeat replies no longer select reasoning payloads as the main visible reply when reasoning is disabled by default.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `1d36bdf2ce876dc18c7644da8e308f049fa23fc8`, Node 24.16.0, production `runHeartbeatOnce`, real temporary `OpenClawConfig`, real session-store JSON file, real plugin registry registration, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` / `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`, and a local Telegram-shaped outbound adapter that records delivery attempts without contacting Telegram.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=1d36bdf2ce876dc18c7644da8e308f049fa23fc8 \
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-hb-proof-home \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-hb-proof-home/state \
node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-heartbeat-proof.ts
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied console output):

```text
OpenClaw heartbeat behavior proof
head=1d36bdf2ce876dc18c7644da8e308f049fa23fc8
[
  {
    "case": "default suppresses late blockquoted reasoning after HEARTBEAT_OK",
    "includeReasoning": false,
    "status": "ran",
    "deliveryCount": 0,
    "deliveredTexts": [],
    "disallowedReasoningDelivered": false,
    "sessionStore": "sessions.json"
  },
  {
    "case": "default sends visible final and ignores later reasoning",
    "includeReasoning": false,
    "status": "ran",
    "deliveryCount": 1,
    "deliveredTexts": [
      "Visible heartbeat update"
    ],
    "disallowedReasoningDelivered": false,
    "sessionStore": "sessions.json"
  },
  {
    "case": "includeReasoning true still delivers reasoning-only output",
    "includeReasoning": true,
    "status": "ran",
    "deliveryCount": 1,
    "deliveredTexts": [
      "Thinking\n\n_private chain of thought_"
    ],
    "disallowedReasoningDelivered": false,
    "sessionStore": "sessions.json"
  }
]
```

- Observed result after fix: With `includeReasoning` unset, a late blockquoted reasoning payload after `HEARTBEAT_OK` produced zero outbound sends, and a visible final heartbeat update followed by reasoning delivered only the visible final text. With `includeReasoning: true`, a reasoning-only response still delivered through the explicit reasoning path.
- What was not tested: No live xAI/OpenAI Responses model and no real Telegram network delivery were used. The model reply ordering and outbound network call were locally controlled to isolate the heartbeat payload-selection behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Live Telegram/provider proof would require credentials and a model response ordering that reproduces the reporter's payload order; this local proof targets the heartbeat runner layer identified as the root cause in #92260.

## Tests and validation

Focused commands run:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/heartbeat-reply-payload.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/reply-payload.test.ts
git diff --check
/Users/harjothk/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/python/bin/python3 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Results:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` passed 3 Vitest shards: 4 selector tests, 44 infra heartbeat runner tests, and 53 plugin-sdk reply-payload tests.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Autoreview initially found two regressions; both were fixed. Final autoreview result: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

Regression coverage added:

- Selector tests cover late flagged reasoning, legacy-prefixed reasoning, blockquoted reasoning, reasoning-only inputs, and scan-through behavior.
- Heartbeat runner tests cover default-hidden late reasoning after `HEARTBEAT_OK`, legacy-prefixed and blockquoted late reasoning, and `includeReasoning: true` reasoning-only delivery.

target.title: fix: surface safe terminal tool fallbacks
target.body:
## Summary

- Add opt-in terminal fallback metadata through agent-core, session tool definitions, subscribe handling, and the embedded runner.
- Surface safe terminal summaries for tool-loop and incomplete-turn exits, while keeping undeclared tool output neutral instead of leaking raw results.
- Declare structured fallbacks for `cron` and `web_fetch`, and keep Docker image builds from carrying stale `dist` output.
- Tighten delivery/side-effect evidence for channel replies, source-reply deliveries, context-engine pairing, compaction ownership, and heartbeat lifecycle handling.

## Contract note

- `terminalSummary` and `terminalResultFallback` are intentionally public tool metadata for safe terminal presentation.
- Tools opt in explicitly; undeclared raw tool output is not surfaced as a fallback.
- Generic tool activity, accepted child-session spawns, messaging sends, cron additions, and replay-invalid aborts continue to suppress fallback model replay so side effects are not duplicated.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.does-not-append-text-end-content-is.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.suppresses-message-end-block-replies-message-tool.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-definition-adapter.after-tool-call.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-definition-adapter.after-tool-call.fires-once.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-owned-tool-runtime-contract.test.ts src/dockerfile.test.ts src/agents/terminal-reply.test.ts packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm test:extensions:package-boundary`
- `pnpm build`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Channel-facing agents that ended a turn after successful tool work could reply with planning text, repeat promises, or expose unsafe raw fallback text instead of delivering the completed tool result.

Real environment tested: A live Discord-backed OpenClaw stack using the patched image and a connected bot. Identifying stack, bot, channel, image, and run IDs are intentionally omitted from this public PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent live prompts that exercised a scheduler/cron path and a `web_fetch` tool-loop timeout path through the running gateway.

Evidence after fix:

Redacted live channel output, scheduler path:

```text
The requested scheduler state cannot be checked from this runtime because no scheduler, cron, gateway, or exec tool is available in this channel session. I cannot claim a live cron check without that tool.
```

Redacted live channel output, `web_fetch` terminal fallback path:

```text
URL: https://example.com
Final URL: https://example.com
Status: 200
Title: Example Domain
Cached: true
Truncated: false
```

Observed result after fix: The channel received user-visible results for both proof cases. The `web_fetch` fallback included URL, final URL, status, title, cache state, and truncation state, with no raw unsafe tool dump. The scheduler path returned a direct capability blocker instead of a promise to perform an unavailable check.

What was not tested: Broad cross-channel E2E beyond Discord was not rerun. One unrelated live runtime metadata drift was observed during proof and is omitted here because it is not part of this patch.

target.title: fix(fireworks): resolve catalog model limits from plugin.json via core
target.body:
**TL;DR:** the two bundled Fireworks Kimi models silently ran with the wrong limits — a not-yet-configured `kimi-k2p6` resolved with the default template's 256,000 context window instead of its own 262,144, and `kimi-k2p5-turbo` could lose its `unsupportedToolSchemaKeywords` compat. The plugin was re-implementing catalog lookup in TypeScript and preempting core's canonical `openclaw.plugin.json` resolution. This PR deletes the parallel path and defers manifest ids to core. ## Summary **What does this fix?** `resolveFireworksDynamicModel` cloned the *default* template for any id not already in user config — flattening per-model `contextWindow`/`maxTokens`/`compat` declared in the manifest. Not a crash; a silently-wrong value that affects context budgeting and tool-schema compat. **What changed?** - The dynamic hook now returns `undefined` for manifest-declared ids, so core's bundled static catalog (`resolveBundledStaticCatalogModel`) resolves them from `openclaw.plugin.json` — one canonical, JSON-driven path. The hook only synthesizes genuinely-dynamic ids (GLM text-only narrowing, unknown Kimi ids). - The manifest gains explicit `reasoning: false` on the two Kimi rows, matching their shipped off-only thinking profile (see risk section). - Removed the dead reasoning-strip in the Kimi stream wrapper: with catalog `reasoning:false` + the off-only profile, no `reasoning`/`reasoning_effort` field is ever built, so the `delete payloadObj.reasoning*` lines were no-ops. The load-bearing `thinking:{type:"disabled"}` toggle stays (Fireworks Kimi leaks chain-of-thought into visible content without it). - `isFireworksGlmModelId` moved to `model-id.ts`; catalog clones via `structuredClone` (deep, covers `compat`). **Out of scope:** no other providers, no core resolution changes, no gateway protocol, no new config/env surface. ## Linked context - **#92217 (same author) is the complementary companion**: it adds four Fireworks reasoning models (DeepSeek V4 Pro, MiniMax M2.7, GLM-5.1, GPT-OSS 120B) with `reasoning_effort` compat and per-family thinking profiles. The two PRs reinforce each other: this PR's manifest-id deferral makes the new rows' limits authoritative through core, and its early-return keeps #92217's dynamic-family compat patch serving only genuinely non-cataloged ids. They conflict textually in 3 files (`index.ts`/`model-id.ts`/`provider-catalog.ts`) where both made near-identical changes (same GLM-helper move, same `structuredClone` direction); whichever lands second rebases mechanically. - Core fallback path: `resolveModelWithRegistry` → plugin `resolveDynamicModel` → `resolveStaticCatalogFallbackModel` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.ts`), gated by `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`. ## Real behavior proof Behavior or issue addressed: bundled Fireworks Kimi models apply their per-model `contextWindow`/`maxTokens`/`compat` from `openclaw.plugin.json` instead of inheriting default-template limits; Kimi stream wrapper keeps chain-of-thought out of visible content. Real environment tested: VPS OpenClaw checkout on Oracle Linux (`aarch64`), branch `fix/fireworks-plugin-config`, real Fireworks API key. Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw capability model inspect --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6 --json` (and the `kimi-k2p5-turbo` equivalent); then live runs: `openclaw capability model run --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6 --prompt "Reply with exactly: ok" --thinking off --json` and the same with `--thinking high`. Evidence after fix: terminal capture and screenshot below. `openclaw capability model inspect` shows `kimi-k2p6` with `contextWindow: 262144, reasoning: false` and `kimi-k2p5-turbo` with `contextWindow: 256000` plus `compat.unsupportedToolSchemaKeywords: ["not"]`; both live `openclaw capability model run` calls (thinking off and high) returned this copied live output with no `…` leak: ```json { "ok": true, "capability": "model.run", "transport": "local", "provider": "fireworks", "model": "accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6", "outputs": [{ "text": "ok", "mediaUrl": null }] } ``` Observed result after fix: each model surfaces its own distinct limits — the default template no longer flattens catalog metadata. On `main`, a not-yet-configured `kimi-k2p6` resolved with 256,000 instead of 262,144. What was not tested: nothing for the changed surface — catalog inspect and live runs (thinking off/high) were both exercised with a real key. Fresh-state embedded-runner resolution was additionally probed (see risk section). ## Tests and validation - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts`: 109 passed, incl. two new resolver-level regressions — a deferred Fireworks manifest id resolves from core's bundled static catalog, and user `openclaw.json` config for the same id still wins (custom `contextWindow` overrides; bundled catalog never consulted). Locks the precedence chain used by every neighbor provider. - `extensions/fireworks` vitest: 13 passed — new "defers manifest catalog models to core static-catalog resolution" test; per-model limits locked from the manifest; off-only thinking-profile coverage retained; the test that only covered the deleted reasoning-strip was removed with it. - `check:test-types`, `tsgo:extensions(+test)`, oxlint, oxfmt: clean. - Autoreview (claude sonnet-4-6, medium): "patch is correct (0.93), no accepted/actionable findings". ## Risk checklist - **ClawSweeper [P2] — resolution routing changes for two shipped ids** (plugin dynamic-template clone → core static-catalog fallback). Addressed three ways: (1) onboarded/configured installs resolve these ids from the model registry (generated from the same manifest) and never reach either fallback; (2) the entry points that can see a bare manifest id without registry coverage enable `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` (`runtime-llm.runtime.ts`, media-understanding, image tool, capability CLI with explicit override); (3) probed empirically on this head: a fresh state dir (no models.json, only the env API key) plus an embedded `openclaw agent --local` turn on deferred `kimi-k2p6` — the path where `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` is conditional — resolves and answers (`READY`). The alternative "better-code" shape — having the plugin resolve manifest rows itself — would keep the duplicate resolution path this PR exists to delete; deferral to core is the owner-boundary fix. - **ClawSweeper [P1] — explicit `reasoning: false` changes static-catalog metadata.** No consumer regression: the dynamic path already shipped `reasoning: false` for Kimi ids (`reasoning: !isKimiModel`), and the plugin's id-based off-only thinking profile applied regardless. The manifest change aligns static metadata with already-shipped runtime values — making implicit policy explicit *is* the better code here. - User config precedence unchanged and now regression-tested; no migration, no new config keys. - Net non-test LOC down (Source +1 overall per ClawSweeper; the rest is tests). ## Current review state What is the next action? Maintainer review — specifically the bot's "accept the static-catalog handoff" decision (its recommended option 1). A `@clawsweeper re-review` is warranted: the posted review was against `1794efbba18d`, superseded by current head `f8d436b14`. What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Maintainer: provider/runtime-owner acceptance of the resolution-order handoff + ordering vs #92217 (whichever lands second rebases 3 files mechanically). Author/CI/external: nothing outstanding. Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Both ClawSweeper risks are answered in the Risk checklist above with code-level analysis: [P2] via the registry/entry-point/fresh-state-probe argument (its option 2 "guard coverage" is satisfied by the new `model.test.ts` regressions plus the live probe), [P1] via the already-shipped-runtime-value argument. The bot's rank-up move (owner confirmation of the handoff) is the open item — that is a maintainer decision, not a code change.
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

A `@clawsweeper re-review` is warranted: the posted review was against `1794efbba18d`, superseded by current head `f8d436b14`. What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Maintainer: provider/runtime-owner acceptance of the resolution-order handoff + ordering vs #92217 (whichever lands second rebases 3 files mechanically). Author/CI/external: nothing outstanding. Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Both ClawSweeper risks are answered in the Risk checklist above with code-level analysis: [P2] via the registry/entry-point/fresh-state-probe argument (its option 2 "guard coverage" is satisfied by the new `model.test.ts` regressions plus the live probe), [P1] via the already-shipped-runtime-value argument. The bot's rank-up move (owner confirmation of the handoff) is the open item — that is a maintainer decision, not a code change.
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Channel echo / session pinning (pin-from-here) with native Telegram rendering A session can mirror its turns to other threads that are bound to the **same** session, so a long run you started in one place can be followed from another — and the mirror renders **natively** in the destination channel (live draft stream, not a flat `[echo]` copy). ### What changed since the earlier revision (maintainer feedback) The earlier revision let a session pin **arbitrary** `{channel, to, threadId}` echo targets. The maintainer's concern was that continuous mirroring to arbitrary chat ids is a heavy primitive and lacks an authorization story for streaming conversation content to any chat id. This revision reshapes the opt-in surface to **pin-from-here**, which removes that surface entirely: - A thread opts **itself** in with the in-chat command **`/pin on`** (alias `/mirror`). It can only mirror the session it is **already bound to** — there is no arbitrary-target argument — so a pin can never cross a session boundary. Every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that exact session. **Zero new authorization surface.** - The operator add paths (`sessions echo` CLI + `sessions.echo` RPC) now **reject any target that is not a verified participant** of the session (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), with guidance to use `/pin` from the target thread instead. The "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path is gone. - Self-exclusion is correct by construction: `/pin` captures the thread's own origin identity (the exact fields the fan-out uses as a turn's origin), so when this thread drives a turn it is excluded from its own fan-out; when a sibling thread of the same session drives, this thread receives the mirror. `last*` (the session's persistent external delivery route) is unchanged — a transient webchat/cron poke still must not hijack an external session's route (session-delivery.ts, #47745). ### Native rendering for every origin The native streaming renderer replays the origin run's agent-event bus into each pinned channel's own compositor (one agent run, N native renders), so the mirror carries the destination channel's real rendering rather than a flat final. **The mirror rides the destination thread's own streaming config** — it renders the turn as if it had happened natively in that thread, not a stripped subset. The mirror resolver already replays the origin run's full event stream (`assistant`/`item`/`tool`/`reasoning`/`plan`/`command`/`patch`) into the target's `GetReplyOptions`, but the Telegram echo renderer previously implemented only `onPartialReply`, so the verbose tool-progress lane was silently dropped regardless of how the destination was configured. The renderer now honors the same callbacks the native compositor honors, gated on the destination account's own config and reusing the shared `buildChannelProgressDraftLineForEntry` / `formatChannelProgressDraftText` / `mergeChannelProgressDraftLine` primitives: in `progress` stream mode with `preview.toolProgress`, the tool-progress lane renders (held for the turn, collapsed to the answer at finalize — matching native progress rendering); in `partial` mode the answer streams alone, as a native group reply would. No toggle — the mirror rides whatever that channel would do. This revision also fixes a latent bug so the native path engages for **non- channel-origin** turns (webchat `chat.send`, cron/heartbeat): the fan-out derived its origin from the session's `last*`, which those turns deliberately do not claim — so the origin defaulted to the previously-active channel and, when that channel was itself the pinned target, the fan-out **self-excluded** it and fell back to the flat post-hoc mirror. It now derives the fan-out origin from the turn's **actual** origin (`OriginatingChannel/To`), falling back to `last*`, so a webchat trigger renders natively in the pinned channel. ### New in this revision (4 commits on top of the existing channel-echo work) - `feat(reply): add /pin (pin-from-here) in-chat command` — `commands-pin.ts` + registration; stores the caller's origin identity into the bound session's echo targets. Unit tests incl. an end-to-end check chaining `/pin` through the real `resolveEchoTargets` fan-out (sibling included, self excluded). - `feat(echo): reject arbitrary-target echo adds; require a session participant` — `targetMatchesSessionParticipant` in `echo.ts`, wired into both operator add paths. Unit tests for accept/reject/fail-closed. - `fix(echo): native streaming fan-out uses the turn's actual origin, not stale last*` — `agent-runner-execution.ts`. - `feat(telegram): echo renderer rides the destination's streaming config` — `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer.ts` (+ `echo-renderer-register.ts` threading the account config/stream mode). Wires the tool-progress lane (`onToolStart`/`onItemEvent`/`onPlanUpdate`/`onCommandOutput`/`onPatchSummary`/ `onApprovalEvent`) the resolver already drives but the renderer ignored; fully contained to the Telegram extension (no core change). New unit tests assert the lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. The existing channel-echo infrastructure (native streaming fan-out, mirror resolver, owner-bound `registerEchoRendererFactory`, the types-only `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath) is otherwise unchanged. ### Tests + CI (current head `0388fad1115`) - `pnpm tsgo:core` + `tsgo:extensions`: 0 errors on the changed surface. `oxlint` on the changed files: 0 warnings / 0 errors. - Unit tests for the changed surface pass, including the **152-test `agent-runner-execution.test`** suite the origin fix touches, plus `commands-pin.test`, `echo.test`, `chat.send-user-echo.test`, and the echo suite (`echo-renderer.test` / `echo-renderer-register.test` / `echo-streaming.test`, 48 tests) — the new echo-renderer tests assert the tool-progress lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow with a local patched crabbox: a real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`OpenClaw 2026.5.31 d6c154c`, global-installed) + Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, run locally — driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with `/verbose on` and an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread and webchat are bound to one session; the thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt, and the pinned thread receives that webchat-origin turn — including the real tool result — rendered natively. Captured sequence (real Telegram Desktop, see image/video below): 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Run the shell command: date -u — use your exec tool, then tell me the UTC time…` — the webchat-origin, tool-using prompt 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The current UTC time is 14:35:12 on Thursday, June 11, 2026."** — i.e. a real `date -u` exec, run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread. A real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` turn (with `/verbose on` and an exec `date -u` tool call) is triggered from webchat and mirrored natively into the pinned Telegram thread — autoplay GIF (left, trimmed to the reply landing) + final frame (right):
Native-path engagement on the same build was confirmed by instrumenting the fan-out: for the webchat-origin turn `resolveEchoTargets` returns the Telegram target (origin = webchat, not self-excluded), the factory is found, and the renderer is **created** (native). Gateway log for the filmed run: `session turn created … channel=webchat` → the real claude turn ran the exec tool (`outcome=completed`) and the result rendered in the pinned thread. Session store after `/pin`: `echoTargets: [{"channel":"telegram","to":"telegram:-1003900553563","threadId":1,"label":"pinned"}]`. #### Riding the destination config — the tool-progress lane renders in the mirror With the destination thread in `progress` stream mode (`/verbose on`, `preview.toolProgress`), the mirror renders the **verbose tool-progress lane**, not just the answer. A real `claude-cli` turn triggered from webchat runs an exec tool (`sleep 4; hostname`) — chosen so the result is not derivable from context, proving a genuine tool call — and the pinned Telegram thread shows the **`🛠️ Bash`** progress lane while the tool runs, then collapses to the answer **`The hostname is crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`** (the real box hostname). Autoplay GIF of the full `prompt → 🛠️ Bash lane → answer` sequence (left) and the tool-lane frame (right):
**Proof environment:** single-node local gateway, one Telegram backend, isolated state dir; real `claude-cli` model with exec tools enabled. Sensitive chat ids are the test supergroup, not production. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). Implementation, tests, and the local behavior-proof capture were AI-assisted; framing and the proof are the author's.

target.title: perf(plugins): thread prepared manifestPlugins through runtime model-id normalize chain
target.body:
## Summary

Extends the prepared-manifest-metadata threading from #82814 into the runtime plugin-normalize branch so callers that already carry prepared `manifestPlugins` skip the residual `installs.json` slot-or-load disk walk in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. Closes the gap that #82814's measurements left visible (Kova `agent-cold-warm-message` was at 28 metadata scans per cold+warm sample post-#82814).

Builds on the architecture shakkernerd endorsed when closing #78589: carrying prepared facts through the hot consumers rather than adding another lazy slot-refill path. Does not touch `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` itself.

## Why

`#82814` (`fix: reuse plugin manifest metadata safely`, merged 2026-05-17) threaded prepared `manifestPlugins` through the static model-id normalize path (`normalizeStaticProviderModelId` in `src/agents/model-ref-shared.ts`) so consumers stop consulting the `current-plugin-metadata-snapshot` single-slot handoff in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. That eliminated most of the hot-path `installs.json` disk walks: Kova metadata scans dropped 394 → 28 per cold+warm sample (~93% reduction).

The remaining 28 scans come from the runtime plugin-normalize branch. `src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts` already receives `options.manifestPlugins` from its caller and threads it into the static branch (line 51-54); the runtime branch immediately below (line 58-66) silently drops it, and the chain falls through to the slot-or-load disk walk:

\`\`\`
src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts:59
  → normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime(...)         (no plugins param)
    → normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin(params)     (no plugins param)
      → normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest(params) (params.plugins undefined)
        → loadManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies   (slot-or-load disk walk)
\`\`\`

`normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest` already honors `params.plugins` to short-circuit:

\`\`\`ts
function loadManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies(params) {
  if (params.plugins) {
    return collectManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies(params.plugins);  // prepared path
  }
  const { snapshot, cacheable } = resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies(params);  // slot-or-load
  // ...
}
\`\`\`

The intermediate functions just never accepted a `plugins` field. This PR adds it as an additive optional param and forwards `options?.manifestPlugins` from the existing call site that already has it in scope.

## How

Three surgical edits:

1. **`src/agents/provider-model-normalization.runtime.ts`** — add optional `plugins?: readonly Pick<PluginManifestRecord, \"modelIdNormalization\">[]` to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime`'s params. The body just forwards `params` unchanged to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin`, so no behavioral change beyond the type signature.

2. **`src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`** — add the same optional `plugins?` field to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin`'s params. The existing `normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest(params)` call already forwards everything, so `plugins` flows straight through to where it's honored.

3. **`src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts:59`** — at the runtime call site, thread `options?.manifestPlugins` into the runtime call. Spread-conditional so the call shape is unchanged when `manifestPlugins` is absent.

Net diff: +6 LOC across three source files (plus tests and changelog).

## Relationship to the previously closed PR #78589

`#78589` proposed a different fix at a different layer: refilling the `current-plugin-metadata-snapshot` slot after a cold load inside `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. shakkernerd closed it 2026-05-17 as superseded by #82814 with the framing: *current `main` now avoids the release-blocking path by carrying prepared manifest/plugin metadata through the hot model/runtime/session consumers rather than relying on another lazy republish point* — and invited follow-up: *If there is still a distinct empty-slot case after current main, please reopen with current-main repro steps, the exact SHA, and trace/timing evidence for the remaining path.*

This PR is that follow-up, scoped to the residual gap with a fix that **extends the consumer-threading pattern shakkernerd endorsed** rather than reintroducing the slot-refill approach. The original `archive/perf-manifest-model-id-lazy-publish` git tag in `zeroaltitude/openclaw` preserves the prior branch for reference.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Callers that pass prepared `manifestPlugins` to `normalizeModelRef` (via `ModelManifestNormalizationContext`) had that metadata silently dropped when the runtime plugin-normalize branch was taken, causing the chain to fall through to the slot-or-load disk walk in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` even though prepared metadata was available in the caller's scope.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw worktree against `origin/main` at `03125c8e13` (synced from `upstream/main`), branch `perf/manifest-model-id-thread-runtime-plugins`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json`
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/agents/model-selection-manifest-workspace.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-model-id-normalization.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/provider-model-normalization.runtime.ts src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: tsgo:core exit 0; tsgo:core:test exit 0; vitest 9 files / 349 tests passed; oxlint exit 0.
- **Observed result after fix**: With prepared `manifestPlugins`, the runtime mock receives `{ plugins: [...] }` and the production chain hands them through to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest`, where the existing `params.plugins` short-circuit in `loadManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies` takes the prepared path and skips `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` entirely. Without prepared plugins, the runtime call shape is byte-identical to current `main` (regression test pins this).
- **Real-runtime proof script (no mocks of the seam under test)**: added `scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts` (latest commit `9f7af8e`). It drives the real `normalizeModelRef` → `normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime` → `manifest-model-id-normalization.ts` chain against a fresh empty `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, instrumenting the disk seam the snapshot path actually depends on **on current main: the SQLite-backed installed-plugin-index store** at `<stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite`, resolved through the real production resolver `resolveInstalledPluginIndexStorePath(...)` → `resolveOpenClawStateSqlitePath(...)`. (`readPersistedInstalledPluginIndexFromSqlite` guards on `existsSync(resolveInstalledPluginIndexStorePath(...))` before opening the state DB; the legacy `<stateDir>/plugins/installs.json` JSON ledger is no longer the live read path.) The sentinel counts touches on that exact SQLite path. Run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts`. Captured local output:

  ```text
  [1/3] without-prepared-plugins-walks-snapshot (control)
      normalizeModelRef result: provider=openai model=gpt-5
      sentinel disk touches on <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite: 9
      touched paths: <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite
      ok
  [2/3] with-prepared-plugins-consults-them-and-skips-snapshot
      normalizeModelRef result: provider=openai model=gpt-5o
      sentinel disk touches on <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite: 6
      touched paths: <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite
      delta vs control: 3 (snapshot path bypassed)
      ok
  [3/3] result-identical-on-both-paths
      with empty plugins:  provider=anthropic model=claude-sonnet-4.6
      without plugins:     provider=anthropic model=claude-sonnet-4.6
      ok

  All runtime assertions passed.
  ```

  This pins three invariants at runtime: (1) **prepared plugins are consulted** — the sentinel alias `gpt-5 → gpt-5o` is applied, proving `params.plugins` reached `collectManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies`; (2) **the snapshot disk walk is bypassed** — strictly fewer touches of the SQLite installed-plugin-index store than the no-plugins control (9 → 6, delta 3 = the per-call probing `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` performs against `<stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite` when no prepared plugins are supplied); (3) **no behavior drift** — an empty plugin list yields a byte-identical normalized id. Proof-script validation: `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts` → All runtime assertions passed; `oxlint` clean, `git diff --check` clean.

  > **Re: ClawSweeper [P2] / `status: 📣 needs proof`** — the prior proof revision targeted `<stateDir>/plugins/` (the legacy JSON ledger), which current main no longer reads, so the control scenario touched it zero times and the "snapshot path probes disk at least once" floor never fired against the real seam. This revision retargets the sentinel to the SQLite installed-plugin-index store path the production snapshot resolution actually probes, so the bypass is now measured against the live disk seam.
- **What was not tested**: the production-scale *magnitude* (the 28 → ~3 scan reduction) under the Kova `agent-cold-warm-message` harness — I do not have the canonical fixture. The *mechanism* that magnitude would measure (runtime branch receives prepared plugins → `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` skipped) is now directly proven at runtime by the proof script above; the production savings scale 1:1 with how often the runtime plugin-normalize branch is hit on cold-slot turns. Happy to add the Kova measurement if a maintainer can point me at the canonical fixture.

## Tests

Added in `src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts`:

- `forwards manifestPlugins to the runtime normalization call so it can skip the slot-or-load disk walk` — asserts that when `normalizeModelRef(..., { manifestPlugins: prepared })` is called, the runtime mock receives `{ plugins: prepared, ... }`.
- `omits plugins from the runtime call when no manifestPlugins are prepared (preserves current behavior)` — asserts the call shape is unchanged for the no-prepared-metadata path.

All existing test cases in the affected files continue to pass.

## Verification

- `tsgo:core`: clean
- `tsgo:core:test`: clean
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <5 affected files>`: 9 files / 349 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <4 affected source files>`: 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check`: clean
- Branched from `origin/main` (= `upstream/main` at `03125c8e13`); no rebase needed at submission.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap auth-rate-limit entries map under unique-IP flood
target.body:
Closes #77986

## Summary

`src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts` keeps an in-memory `entries: Map<scope:ip, RateLimitEntry>` of failed auth attempts. There is a periodic `setInterval(prune)` (default 60s) that walks the Map and drops entries with empty attempts, but **no hard size cap**. Between prune ticks, an unauthenticated attacker rotating source IPs can push one fresh entry per IP, scaling memory use with `attacker request rate × pruneIntervalMs`.

This change adds:

- `maxEntries` config (default 10,000) on `RateLimitConfig`.
- An `enforceMaxEntries` pass on new inserts in `recordFailure` that drops oldest entries by `Map` insertion order.
- **Locked-out entries are skipped during eviction** so an attacker cannot escape a lockout by flooding fresh failures to evict their own locked entry — the lockout state is the whole point of the rate limiter.

The existing periodic prune, sliding window, lockout, and reset semantics are unchanged.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.test.ts` → 24/24 pass including the two new cases (`caps entries via FIFO drop when a flood of unique IPs exceeds maxEntries`, `preserves locked-out entries during flood eviction`).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` clean.
- Live tsx runtime proof included below in **Real behavior proof**.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: the entries Map had no hard cap; under sustained unique-IP attack memory use grew with attacker bandwidth × pruneIntervalMs (default 60s) before the next prune could compact it.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22 runtime against the patched `auth-rate-limit.ts` module via `pnpm exec tsx`. No mocks, no test framework. Drove the real `recordFailure` / `check` API with synthetic IPs.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm exec tsx /tmp/auth-rate-limit-cap-proof.mts`. Two scenarios: (1) 50,000 unique-IP failures with `maxEntries=1000`, and (2) two pre-locked entries plus a 200-IP flood with `maxEntries=4` to verify locked entries survive.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```
=== unique-IP flood (cap=1000, no auto-prune) ===
  recorded      0 failures  size=1
  recorded   5000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  10000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  15000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  20000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  25000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  30000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  35000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  40000 failures  size=1000
  recorded  45000 failures  size=1000
final size after 50k unique-IP failures: 1000

=== lockout-preservation under flood (cap=4) ===
pre-flood size: 2, 10.0.50.1 locked: true
post-flood size: 4, cap=4
10.0.50.1 still locked: true
10.0.50.2 still locked: true
```

- **Observed result after fix**: cap holds at 1000 across 50k unique-IP failures (oldest non-locked entries evicted FIFO). With cap=4 and 2 pre-locked entries, a 200-IP flood pins size at 4 and both locked entries survive — verifying eviction preserves lockouts.
- **What was not tested**: behavior against a real public-internet attack stream. The fix is a `Map` cap on a deterministic insert path; the runtime demo exercises the same `recordFailure` path real attackers would hit, just at higher rates than a unit test can.

## Notes for reviewer

- The `maxEntries` cap, periodic prune, and sliding window compose: prune drops entries with empty windows, sliding window drops old timestamps, cap drops oldest non-locked entries on overflow. Each handles a different failure mode.
- 10,000 default chosen to comfortably fit a real gateway's tracked-IP working set (any single openclaw deployment) while still bounding memory use to ~1MB worst-case for the attempt arrays.
- Part of the memory-leak audit sweep following #77952 (Discord cache) and #77973 (agent-job cache); same shape on a different surface.

target.title: fix(gateway): surface OAuth auth URLs in wizard protocol
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: RPC wizard clients could ask users to paste an OAuth redirect URL without receiving the authorization URL in the wizard protocol.
- Why it matters: Windows/remote wizard clients cannot reliably complete OAuth setup when the URL only appears in gateway stdout or logs.
- What changed: Added optional structured `step.auth` metadata for wizard text steps and wired OAuth redirect URLs through shared provider flows, OpenAI Codex, Chutes, and Gemini.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): API-key/token paste flows and existing device-code note flows remain unchanged; CLI logging/browser-open behavior is preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: OAuth redirect auth flows now include the authorization URL in `wizard.start` / `wizard.next` text-step metadata via optional `step.auth`, so protocol clients can show an Open/Copy URL affordance next to the redirect paste prompt.
Real environment tested: Windows development worktree for OpenClaw gateway/protocol/provider code on Node/pnpm; no live Windows companion app or real provider OAuth credentials were used.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src\gateway\protocol\wizard.schema.test.ts src\plugins\provider-openai-codex-oauth.test.ts src\plugins\provider-oauth-flow.test.ts src\commands\auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test && pnpm lint:core && pnpm lint:extensions`; `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Focused protocol/provider tests assert `step.auth.url`, `step.auth.kind`, and provider metadata for shared OAuth flows, plus direct coverage that RPC wizard prompters force protocol-visible manual auth presentation.
Observed result after fix: Focused Vitest run passed 41 tests / 5 files; typecheck and lint lanes passed; `git diff --check` passed.
What was not tested: Manual Windows companion app end-to-end OAuth flow, real provider OAuth browser login, and full repository `pnpm check` / `pnpm test`.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Prior behavior only emitted OAuth URLs through `runtime.log` / gateway stdout while the protocol step only asked for a redirect URL.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: OAuth redirect flows logged authorization URLs to runtime output but did not carry the URL in the wizard step returned to RPC clients.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered local CLI/provider OAuth behavior but not protocol-visible auth metadata for RPC wizard clients.
- Contributing context (if known): Gateway local/remote detection is insufficient for RPC clients such as Windows/WSL, where the client needs to render the auth challenge even if the gateway environment appears local.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/protocol/wizard.schema.test.ts`, `src/plugins/provider-oauth-flow.test.ts`, `src/plugins/provider-openai-codex-oauth.test.ts`, `src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`, `src/wizard/session.test.ts`, `extensions/google/oauth.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: OAuth redirect text steps include structured `auth` metadata, shared provider handlers include provider ids when known, unknown auth schema fields/kinds are rejected, and RPC wizard prompters present auth challenges manually.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers the protocol schema, wizard prompter seam, shared OAuth helper, and representative provider integrations without requiring live OAuth credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A; new focused coverage was added.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

RPC wizard clients can now render OAuth authorization URLs from `step.auth.url` on text steps instead of relying on gateway stdout/logs. Terminal CLI users still see the same logged URLs and prompts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
OAuth provider -> gateway runtime.log(url) -> RPC client only sees "paste redirect URL"

After:
OAuth provider -> wizard text step auth.url -> RPC client shows auth URL + paste box -> user completes login
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. The authorization URL was already logged for users; this change carries it through structured protocol metadata. Redirect URLs containing authorization codes remain user input, not `step.auth` metadata.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm OpenClaw worktree
- Model/provider: OpenAI Codex OAuth, Chutes OAuth, Google Gemini CLI OAuth seams via focused tests
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway wizard RPC protocol
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Start an RPC wizard-backed provider OAuth login flow that uses redirect-URL paste.
2. Observe the returned text step.
3. Confirm the step includes `auth.kind = "oauth-redirect"` and `auth.url` with provider metadata when known.

### Expected

- The protocol-visible text step carries structured auth metadata for clients to render.

### Actual

- Focused tests confirm `auth` metadata is attached for shared OAuth handlers, Codex, Chutes, and Gemini seams.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Protocol schema accepts structured auth metadata and rejects unknown auth fields/kinds; shared OAuth helper carries URL/kind/provider; Codex and Chutes pass provider ids; RPC wizard prompters force protocol-visible auth presentation; Gemini still forwards auth URL metadata.
- Edge cases checked: Provider field remains optional and omitted when unknown; local manual fallback reuses the last auth URL; CLI prompter remains unaffected because it ignores unknown text params.
- What you did **not** verify: Live Windows companion app OAuth UX, live browser OAuth with real credentials, full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Protocol clients might accidentally depend on `auth.provider` being present.
  - Mitigation: `provider` remains optional in schema/type and tests cover omission when unknown; clients should key rendering from `auth.kind` and `auth.url`.
- Risk: OAuth URLs become visible in an additional protocol field.
  - Mitigation: These URLs were already intentionally printed to runtime logs for user action and do not contain the returned authorization code; secret-bearing redirect input is not placed in `step.auth`.

AI-assisted: Yes. Implemented with Copilot-assisted sessions and reviewed by a separate adversarial Opus 4.8 session before PR creation.

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
Summary:
- Adds structured external-content provenance to tool-call policy hook types.
- Propagates provenance through HookContext, before_tool_call plugin events, trusted tool policy events, and tool hook context.
- Detects EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT markers from the active embedded run prompt/session context.
- Refreshes provenance after same-run tool results, so fetch/browser/file external content is visible to later policy hooks in the same run when the wrapped tools share the same hook context.
- Documents the public hook field and source semantics.
- Refreshes the plugin SDK type barrel so extension package-boundary cache keys invalidate when hook contract shapes change.

Why:
- External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text.
- Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary did not receive structured provenance showing whether external content was present.
- Policy plugins could see the tool name, params, tool kind/input kind, run/toolCallId, derived paths, and session context, but not whether the tool call followed browser, web_fetch, email, webhook, file-fetch, or other untrusted external context.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Same-run external content provenance reaches the later before_tool_call policy boundary after a web_fetch result introduces wrapped EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT. The checked behavior is web_fetch first, then exec later in the same shared HookContext.

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on branch fix/tool-call-external-provenance at PR head e8800a11833801434e6d48a4a0b3f9b03d06d958, running on Linux with Node v24.15.0 and pnpm 11.2.2. The proof used the real active OpenClaw plugin registry, a real trusted tool policy entry, and the real wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook runtime path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Ran a live `node --import tsx --eval ...` harness in the PR worktree. The harness registered a trusted tool policy in the active plugin registry, wrapped a web_fetch tool and a bash/exec tool with wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook, executed web_fetch first, then executed exec in the same shared hook context.

Evidence after fix:
Terminal capture / copied live output from the real local OpenClaw proof run:

```text
[proof] pr=91800
[proof] head_sha=e8800a11833801434e6d48a4a0b3f9b03d06d958
[proof] using real wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook runtime path
[proof] trusted policy count=1
[proof] shared HookContext initially has externalContent=null
[proof-policy] tool=web_fetch toolCallId=proof-web-fetch-call event.externalContent=null ctx.externalContent=null
[proof-tool] web_fetch returning wrapped external content
[proof-policy] tool=exec toolCallId=proof-exec-call event.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} ctx.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}
[proof-tool] exec would run redacted command
[proof-result] first_call=web_fetch externalContent=null status=PASS
[proof-result] second_call=exec event.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} ctx.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} status=PASS
[proof-result] PASS: same-run external web_fetch content reached later before_tool_call policy boundary through wrapped tool execution
```

Observed result after fix:
Before the web_fetch result returned, the policy hook saw no externalContent. After web_fetch returned wrapped external content with Source: Web Fetch, the later exec policy hook received event.externalContent and ctx.externalContent as {"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}. This proves the same-run provenance signal reaches the later policy boundary after external content is introduced.

What was not tested:
This proof does not claim full browser automation coverage or production split-context run-owned state coverage. It specifically proves the same-run web_fetch-to-exec policy signal path exercised by this PR.

## Validation

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts
- pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
- git diff --check

target.title: fix(agents): normalize mangled tool names and IDs from OpenAI-compati…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Some OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Kimi) send tool call IDs like `functions.exec:0` as `functions exec:0` (space instead of dot) and tool names like `exec` as `functions exec`. Tool result matching fails, causing "Tool functions exec not found" errors.
- **Why it matters:** Users on Kimi and similar providers cannot complete tool-using flows; matching is done by ID and name, and the mangled forms did not match.
- **What changed:** Added `normalizeMangledToolCallId()` in `tool-call-id.ts` to rewrite `functions ` -> `functions.` in IDs; use it in `extractToolResultId()` and as the key in `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist()` so mangled and canonical IDs map to the same sanitized value. Added `normalizePrefixedToolName()` in `session-transcript-repair.ts` to strip `functions.` / `functions ` from tool names; applied in assistant block handling, `hasToolCallName`, and `normalizeToolResultName()` so tool-result names are normalized and pairing works.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No change to provider clients or wire format; only transcript repair and ID sanitization for matching.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39091
- Related: normalizeToolResultName fix included

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool-using flows with OpenAI-compatible providers that mangle tool IDs/names (e.g. Kimi) now match tool results to tool calls correctly; "Tool functions exec not found" is resolved.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Provider: OpenAI-compatible (e.g. Kimi) that sends `functions exec:0` / `functions exec` style IDs and names

### Steps

1. Run agent with such a provider; trigger a tool call (e.g. exec).
2. Before fix: tool result may not match, "Tool functions exec not found" or similar.
3. After fix: IDs and names normalize; pairing and sanitization map mangled and canonical forms to the same value.

### Expected

Tool results pair with assistant tool calls; no spurious "tool not found" from ID/name mangling.

### Actual

As expected after the fix.

## Evidence

- `normalizeMangledToolCallId` and `extractToolResultId` / `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist` tests in `tool-call-id.test.ts`.
- `sanitizeToolUseResultPairing` test with mangled ID and prefixed name in `session-transcript-repair.test.ts`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Unit tests added; logic follows issue and Greptile feedback (compare original rawToolName in normalizeToolResultName, use normalized ID as map key).
- **Edge cases checked:** Empty string, already-canonical IDs, names without prefix.
- **What you did not verify:** Live Kimi or other provider E2E.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert the commit.
- Files: `src/agents/tool-call-id.ts`, `src/agents/session-transcript-repair.ts`.

## Risks and Mitigations

**None.** Normalization is additive and only affects matching/sanitization; no new execution paths.

target.title: fix(agents): carry provenance through active subagent wakes
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #91356, #91330, #91333.

Carry typed `inputProvenance` through active subagent wake steering so resumed requester turns keep an explicit inter-session provenance signal instead of arriving as untyped queued messages.

This is intentionally narrower than the closed #91333 approach. It does not suppress automatic-mode finals based on ordinary `message(action="send")` receipts, and it does not infer terminal source-reply ownership from `messageId`, target matching, or prose.

## Why

The maintainer feedback on #91330/#91333 clarified the unsafe boundary: a successful send proves delivery, but it does not prove that the send was the terminal source reply. That replacement needs an explicit terminal-source-reply contract and thread identity; this PR does not attempt that.

This patch only handles the active wake side: when a subagent completion wakes an active requester session, the runtime already knows this is an inter-session wake. The queued steering message should carry that typed provenance through the active run / reply-run bridge so downstream routing and final-selection logic can reason from explicit metadata instead of an untyped internal wake.

## Changes

- Add active-session steering options for `inputProvenance`.
- Preserve the existing `AgentSession.steer(text, images)` call shape while allowing `steer(text, { images, inputProvenance })`.
- Apply provenance to queued steering user messages with `applyInputProvenanceToUserMessage`.
- Carry queue options through reply-run fallback queueing without changing the legacy no-options call shape.
- Pass `subagent_announce` / completion provenance through active requester wake queue options.
- Add regression coverage for active wake, retry/best-effort, stale-thread handoff, generated-media wake, and reply-run fallback paths.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: active subagent completion wakes could cross the requester-session boundary as untyped steering messages, even though the runtime already knows the wake is an inter-session `subagent_announce`/completion handoff.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `c0d4598971eb73a7d6b0787fa3b0cb09b21bb592` on branch `agent/xiaozhua/active-wake-provenance-clean`. The after-fix runtime proof used a temporary OpenClaw test state/config on macOS with channels/cron/browser/canvas disabled and exercised the real `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` active-wake dispatch path. The final queue sink was instrumented only to capture the active embedded-run queue options, so no private channel, model, or credential was required.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
# Runtime trace from the PR head; temporary proof script only, not committed.
OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=$(git rev-parse HEAD)   node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-proof/pr92251-runtime-proof.mjs | tee /tmp/pr92251-runtime-proof.out

# Focused regression and CI-style gates run locally on the same PR head.
git diff --check
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm tsgo:prod
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm check:test-types
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
PR_92251_ACTIVE_WAKE_RUNTIME_PROOF_BEGIN
{
  "command": "node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-proof/pr92251-runtime-proof.mjs",
  "gitHead": "c0d4598971eb73a7d6b0787fa3b0cb09b21bb592",
  "stateLabel": "pr-92251-active-wake-proof",
  "workspaceBasename": "workspace",
  "result": {
    "delivered": true,
    "path": "steered",
    "deliveredAt": 1765460000100,
    "enqueuedAt": 1765460000000,
    "phases": [
      {
        "phase": "steer-primary",
        "delivered": true,
        "path": "steered",
        "deliveredAt": 1765460000100,
        "enqueuedAt": 1765460000000
      }
    ]
  },
  "queueCallCount": 1,
  "queueCall": {
    "sessionId": "runtime-proof-active-session",
    "text": "redacted child completion payload",
    "options": {
      "deliveryTimeoutMs": 120000,
      "inputProvenance": {
        "kind": "inter_session",
        "sourceSessionKey": "agent:subagent:redacted-child",
        "sourceChannel": "webchat",
        "sourceTool": "subagent_announce"
      },
      "steeringMode": "all",
      "debounceMs": 0,
      "waitForTranscriptCommit": true
    }
  }
}
PR_92251_ACTIVE_WAKE_RUNTIME_PROOF_END
```

Additional local gate evidence from the same PR head:

```text
git diff --check: passed
pnpm tsgo:prod: passed
pnpm check:test-types: passed
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile: passed
pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled: Found 0 warnings and 0 errors on 5904 files
```

Observed result after fix: the active requester wake stayed on the `steered` path and queued exactly one active embedded-run message. The captured queue options include explicit `inputProvenance` with `kind: "inter_session"`, `sourceSessionKey: "agent:subagent:redacted-child"`, `sourceChannel: "webchat"`, and `sourceTool: "subagent_announce"`, plus `steeringMode: "all"` and `waitForTranscriptCommit: true`. This shows the runtime handoff now carries provenance through the active wake boundary instead of sending an untyped steering message.

What was not tested: no live Telegram/Slack/bncr end-to-end run was performed on this machine because Docker is not installed here and no provider API env vars are present in the shell (`OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_API_KEY` absent). #91356 was closed as not reproducible in current core, so this PR remains framed as route/provenance boundary hardening rather than a claimed live-channel product fix.

target.title: fix(google): strip provider prefix from Vertex model path
target.body:
## Summary

`buildGoogleVertexRequestUrl` (`extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`) embeds `model.id` into the Vertex request URL with only URL-encoding. A provider-qualified id such as `google/gemini-2.5-flash` therefore produces an invalid path segment:

`.../publishers/google/models/google%2Fgemini-2.5-flash:generateContent` → Vertex rejects it.

The sibling Generative-AI path, `resolveGoogleModelPath`, already strips this prefix via `stripGoogleProviderPrefix`. This mirrors that on the Vertex path (one line; the helper is already imported). Companion to #91125, which added the same strip to the Generative-AI path.

## Verification

- Regression test mirroring the existing Vertex stream test: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts` → 64 passed.
- `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean on the changed files.
- **Live Google Vertex run — see Real behavior proof below.**

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A provider-qualified Google Vertex model id (`google/gemini-2.5-flash`) built an invalid `publishers/google/models/google%2F...` request path that Vertex rejects; the prefix is now stripped so the request reaches the endpoint.

Real environment tested: Live Google Vertex AI (`us-central1`, ADC auth via `gcloud auth application-default login`; project id redacted as `<PROJECT>`). Tested both a direct REST call and OpenClaw's real `createGoogleVertexTransportStreamFn`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Direct Vertex REST call to each path:
```
# Provider-qualified id in the path (what current main builds):
POST https://us-central1-aiplatform.googleapis.com/v1/projects/<PROJECT>/locations/us-central1/publishers/google/models/google%2Fgemini-2.5-flash:generateContent
  -> HTTP 400  { "error": { "message": "Invalid Endpoint name: projects/<PROJECT>/locations/us-central1/publishers/google/models/google%2Fgemini-2.5-flash." } }

# Prefix stripped (what this PR builds):
POST https://us-central1-aiplatform.googleapis.com/v1/projects/<PROJECT>/locations/us-central1/publishers/google/models/gemini-2.5-flash:generateContent
  -> HTTP 200  candidates[0].content.parts[0].text = "ok"
```

2. Ran the real OpenClaw transport (`createGoogleVertexTransportStreamFn`) against live Vertex with model id `google/gemini-2.5-flash`, before vs after this patch:
```
BEFORE (current main, no strip):  stream result text = ""    (request rejected -- invalid endpoint)
AFTER  (this patch):              stream result text = "ok"  (live generation)
```

Evidence after fix: the running OpenClaw Vertex transport returns a live generation ("ok") for a provider-qualified `google/`-prefixed model; the same id on current main returns empty because Vertex 400s the `google%2F` path (explicit `Invalid Endpoint name` error shown above).

Observed result after fix: the Vertex request path no longer contains `google%2F` and reaches the expected Vertex endpoint with HTTP 200; behavior now matches the Generative-AI path.

What was not tested: only a short non-streaming-shaped reply was exercised; no multi-turn or tool-call flow (out of scope for a URL-path fix).
